So the code would look like:

class Solution {
    public String greatestLetter(String s) {
        Set<Character> present = new HashSet<>();
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            present.add(c);
        }
        for (char c = 'Z'; c >= 'A'; c--) {
            if (present.contains(c) && present.contains(Character.toLowerCase(c))) {
                return String.valueOf(c);
            }
        }
        return "";
    }
}

Wait, but Character.toLowerCase(c) for a uppercase letter is the lowercase version. So for example, for c='A', it returns 'a'. So checking if both 'A' and 'a' are present.

Yes.

For example, if a letter is present in lowercase and uppercase, then for the uppercase check, when we check the uppercase version (say 'A'), the code will check if 'A' and 'a' are present. So even if the original has 'a' and 'A', the code will find it when checking 'A'.

So this approach works.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public String greatestLetter(String s) {
        Set<Character> present = new HashSet<>();
        for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
            present.add(c);
        }
        for (char c = 'Z'; c >= 'A'; c--) {
            if (present.contains(c) && present.contains(Character.toLowerCase(c))) {
                return String.valueOf(c);
            }
        }
        return "";
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses a set to track all characters in the string, then checks each uppercase letter from Z to A. If both the uppercase and lowercase forms exist in the set, it returns the uppercase letter. This ensures the greatest valid letter is found efficiently. Strengths include linear time processing and minimal space usage. Weaknesses: None significant for the problem constraints.
